data.title: President Trump Blinks For Now, But Tariffs Remain High
data.description:
On 9 April, President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on the higher “add-on” reciprocal tariffs on 50-plus countries that had been announced the previous week, precipitating a historic equity market rally and showing that there was seemingly a limit to how far he would go to move forward with his trade agenda.

While the worst-case scenario has likely been avoided, meaningful tariffs remain, which are likely to have implications for U.S. and global growth as well as inflation. They include:

  • A 10% universal tariff on all countries, effective April 5. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent indicated 10% would likely be the floor for tariffs going forward, and National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett suggested that it would be a very “high bar” for countries to secure a deal below that 10% level.
  • A 145% tariff on Chinese goods. Trump has indicated there could be a deal with China that could lower tariffs from the punitive 145% rate, but we expect tariffs on China to remain elevated, regardless.
  • A 25% tariff on non-United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA)-compliant goods from Mexico and Canada, effective March 4. Compliant goods from Canada and Mexico are estimated at about 50% of all goods (and have a 0% tariff under USMCA). Thus, we expect more Mexican and Canadian exporters will seek compliance for their non-USMCA compliant goods, which are now subject to tariffs.
  • A 25% tariff on goods imported from any country that imports Venezuelan oil, effective 2 April.
  • A 25% tariff on aluminum and steel, effective 11 March.
  • A 25% tariff on autos (effective 3 April) and auto parts. Similar product tariffs are expected shortly on pharmaceuticals and semiconductors.

These tariffs could represent an increase in the average effective tariff rate of up to about 23% on U.S. imports, certainly not as significant as the approximately 30%-plus the market had expected, but much higher than the average 3% effective tariff rate on imports pre-Trump 2.0. (Note, the effective tariff rate could decline if imports from China collapse, but that would likely entail significant economic friction in and of itself.)

Tariffs and economic growth

The 10% universal tariff rate represents a possible headwind to U.S. growth of up to 1 percentage point and a similar possible impact on inflation (depending on foreign exchange adjustments, among other factors). See my colleague Tiffany Wilding’s piece, “The U.S. Economy’s Trajectory Amid Higher Tariffs.”

The market had been looking for a signal that Trump would blink from the most draconian actions taken last week, and this was certainly it – and it came as a surprise to many in the administration. Indeed, Trump’s Trade Representative, Jamieson Greer, was testifying before the House Committee on Ways and Means and seemingly did not know the announcement was coming. Trump said that the temporary relief had to do in part with the bond market, “where people were getting a little queasy,” and that people on the Hill were getting “yippy” and “afraid.” At the same time, he still reinforced how committed he is to rebalancing trade and how tariffs are overall a very effective tool.

The long-term effects of tariff policies

Over the next 90 days, we should expect to see the White House announce several deals with various countries (India, Australia, Argentina, the U.K., and possibly Japan seems like the low-hanging fruit). While we could see still higher add-on tariffs (in addition to the 10% tariff roll-on after 90 days for countries that do not secure deals), our expectation is that any add-on tariffs would be significantly less draconian than those announced last week. Nonetheless, we should not underestimate Trump’s ideological commitment to this issue, so while the worst may have been avoided, trade policy uncertainty – and significantly higher tariffs and its attendant growth effects – should be priced-in going forward.

Our baseline scenario for the ultimate destination had been a 10% universal tariff on all countries, higher tariffs on China (though lower than 145%), and specific product tariffs (autos, steel, pharma, etc.). But we thought it would take longer, and include more pain, to get there (see my 7 April piece, "Tariff Turbulence: What to Watch, Including Possible Constraints)." While we could see additional, targeted tariffs increase in 90 days, we think Trump and his advisors have touched the hot stove and backed away from the most draconian approach, although tariff threats and uncertainty on countries with the biggest trade deficits are likely to continue (the EU and Vietnam still seem more vulnerable).

With that said, tariff levels, even after Wednesday’s "blink," could have significant implications for growth and inflation. Yet irrespective of those effects, we think there is a stickiness behind the 10% baseline tariff, sector tariffs, and elevated China tariffs for the longer-term (i.e., they are not likely to be rolled-off).

data.title: Air Products And Chemicals: Compelling Opportunity At An Attractive Discount
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Aristotle Capital Value Equity Composite Q1 2025 Commentary.

Air Products and Chemicals, Inc.

Founded in 1940 and with headquarters in Pennsylvania, Air Products and Chemicals (NYSE:APD) is a leading global supplier of industrial gases, including oxygen, nitrogen, helium, hydrogen and others. These essential gases serve critical roles across a wide range of industries, such as refining, chemicals, metals, electronics, manufacturing, healthcare, and food manufacturing and packaging. Air Products is the leading global supplier of hydrogen, with a robust distribution network across North America.

Roughly 50% of the company’s revenue is generated through onsite delivery. This method usually entails 15- to 20-year contracts where Air Products builds a facility at the customer’s site or nearby (or delivers the gases through pipeline systems). These long-term contracts tend to include pass-through and take-or-pay provisions, which provide stability and predictability of cash flows. The company’s merchant gases (roughly 35% of revenue) are delivered in bulk by tanker in either liquid or gas form, usually under five-year contracts, providing a steady but more variable revenue stream. Smaller quantities can also be delivered to customers, usually packaged in cylinders. (This business represents less than 15% of revenue.)

Over the last several years, Air Products has pursued opportunities in clean hydrogen, including the construction of two megaprojects in Saudi Arabia ('NEOM') and Louisiana. Unlike its traditional model, these projects were started without offtake agreements, a shift that raised concerns. Amid delays and rising costs, activist investor Mantle Ridge took a stake in the company, advocating for a more disciplined approach to capital allocation and succession planning. In response, Eduardo Menezes joined as CEO from Linde (LIN), where he led the company’s EMEA operations. Mr. Menezes quickly refocused the company by divesting non-core assets and exiting three projects outside its industrial gases and hydrogen expertise. We view these changes positively, as they enhance strategic focus and align the company with its core strengths.

High-Quality Business

Some of the quality characteristics we have identified for Air Products include:

  • Attractive oligopoly industry with high barriers to entry and high switching costs;
  • Global scale, network of production facilities and distribution infrastructure, as well as engineering expertise;
  • Mission-critical products that represent a small fraction of a client’s costs. As such, clients are willing to pay a premium and sign long-term contracts to ensure uninterrupted operations, leading to consistent FREE cash flow generation over time;
  • Favorable business mix relative to peers. Higher proportion of onsite production, which can be more profitable and predictable than merchant and packaged gases; and
  • History of steady shareholder returns as demonstrated by 43 years of consecutive dividend growth.

Attractive Valuation

While normalizing cash earnings power is always a focus at Aristotle Capital, it is especially important when valuing a capital-intensive business like Air Products. The company is currently in a heavy investment period, with large projects underway. To arrive at an estimate of intrinsic value, in a normal environment, we believe capital expenditures will moderate to just 18% of sales. Moreover, we estimate a modest return of 12% on all projects fully contracted and under construction. Under these key assumptions and others, we purchased shares of Air Products at an attractive discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Compelling Catalysts

  • Refocused strategy and portfolio optimization. Recent divestitures of non-core businesses, including the sale of its LNG process technology, allow management to focus on industrial gases and clean hydrogen, reinforcing the company’s core strengths;
  • New and experienced leadership. New CEO Eduardo Menezes brings deep industry expertise from competitor Linde, where he led the EMEA practice and was responsible for operations in more than 40 countries. His leadership seeks to refocus the company on higher-return projects with clearer paths to operational success; and
  • Megaprojects nearing completion. Completion of the green/blue hydrogen megaprojects (NEOM and Louisiana) should significantly enhance earnings and FREE cash flow generation in the years ahead amid increased demand driven by decarbonization polices in Europe and Asia (Japan and Korea).

data.title: Aristotle Capital Value Equity Composite Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

Sources: CAPS CompositeHubTM, BloombergPast performance is not indicative of future results. Aristotle Value Equity Composite returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses. Aristotle Capital Composite returns are preliminary pending final account reconciliation. Please see important disclosures at the end of this document.

The U.S. equity market began 2025 with a modest decline, with the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) falling 4.27% during the first quarter. In contrast, bonds provided a measure of stability, as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

From a style perspective, the Russell 1000 Value Index outperformed its growth counterpart by 12.11%. On a sector basis, eight out of the eleven sectors within the Russell 1000 Value Index posted positive returns. The best-performing sectors were Energy, Communication Services and Health Care, while Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary and Industrials were the worst.

U.S. economic data reported during the quarter presented a mixed picture. Real GDP growth slowed to an annualized rate of 2.4%, while inflation remained stable. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 2.8% year-over-year in February, reflecting moderate inflationary pressures. Meanwhile, the labor market remained resilient, with the unemployment rate hovering around 4%. However, consumer strength showed signs of strain, as retail sales slowed from levels seen late last year, potentially impacted by adverse weather and broader macroeconomic uncertainty.

Trade policy was a major source of uncertainty during the quarter. President Trump announced a series of new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico and China, citing concerns over illegal immigration, drug trafficking and intellectual property theft. Targeted industries included autos, steel, aluminum and energy, particularly Venezuelan oil. While tariffs initially raised concerns, the administration’s selective enforcement and flexible implementation approach helped ease market anxiety. In response to the evolving economic landscape, the Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25% to 4.50%. The central bank acknowledged potential inflationary pressures from tariffs and moderated expectations for economic growth in 2025.

Despite broader economic headwinds, corporate earnings remained strong. S&P 500 companies reported impressive 17.8% year-over-year earnings growth, the highest rate since 2021. However, tariff-related uncertainties loomed large, with more than 220 companies referencing tariffs in their earnings calls and nearly 15% issuing negative earnings guidance.

On the domestic front, a government shutdown was averted, as President Trump signed a six-month funding bill. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer supported the measure, believing that a shutdown would have allowed the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to terminate government services at a faster rate.

Geopolitically, the U.S. continued its mediation efforts in the Middle East and Ukraine. While a temporary ceasefire agreement was reached between Israel and Hamas in January, tensions flared again in March over disputes regarding hostage releases in Gaza. In Ukraine, U.S. aid was briefly paused following a contentious White House meeting between presidents Trump and Zelensky. Financial and intelligence support resumed after Ukraine signaled it was open to a ceasefire and agreed to revisit terms of a potential mineral deal, aiming to offset the costs of U.S. assistance.

Performance and Attribution Summary

For the first quarter of 2025, Aristotle Capital’s Value Equity Composite posted a total return of 0.78% gross of fees (0.73% net of fees), underperforming the 2.14% return of the Russell 1000 Value Index and outperforming the -4.27% return of the S&P 500 Index. Please refer to the table for detailed performance.

Performance (%) 1Q25 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years
Value Equity Composite (gross) 0.78 1.32 6.57 16.46 11.18
Value Equity Composite ('net') 0.73 1.08 6.31 16.17 10.86
Russell 1000 Value Index 2.14 7.18 6.64 16.15 8.79
S&P 500 Index -4.27 8.25 9.06 18.59 12.50
Source: FactSetPast performance is not indicative of future results. Attribution results are based on sector returns which are gross of investment advisory fees. Attribution is based on performance that is gross of investment advisory fees and includes the reinvestment of income.

The portfolio’s underperformance relative to the Russell 1000 Value Index in the first quarter can be attributed to both allocation effects and security selection. Security selection in Financials, an overweight in Information Technology and an underweight in Health Care detracted the most from relative performance. Conversely, security selection in Consumer Discretionary, Utilities and Energy contributed. (Relative weights are the result of bottom-up security selection.)

Contributors and Detractors for 1Q 2025

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
SONY Blackstone (bX)
Amgen (AMGN) Microsoft (MSFT)
American International Group (AIG) Ameriprise Financial (AMP)
American Water Works (AWK) Lennar (LEN)
Mitsubishi UFJ Financial (MUFG) Adobe (ADBE)

Adobe, the leading provider of content creation and publishing software, was a notable detractor during the quarter. This came despite the company reporting record revenue of over $5.7 billion in the first quarter—a 10% year-over-year increase, with double-digit increases across both its Digital Media and Digital Experience segments. The disconnect between strong fundamentals and share price weakness reflects ongoing market concerns around intensifying competitive threats from generative AI and lower-cost design platforms. Market sentiment has remained cautious around the perceived disruption risk posed by new AI-driven entrants, including OpenAI’s Sora for video generation and platforms like Canva, which cater to the broader prosumer and small and medium-sized business segment. However, we continue to view these as largely non-overlapping with Adobe’s core base of creative professionals, enterprises and agencies—audiences that demand precision, control and integration within larger workflows. Canva, while expanding its feature set, remains limited in its enterprise readiness and depth. Sora, meanwhile, remains early-stage and experimental, with limited commercial application at this point. Crucially, Adobe is not standing still. The company is actively embedding generative AI across its ecosystem through Firefly, which is commercially safe (i.e., free of copyrighted sources to train its models) and integrated natively into Creative Cloud applications like Photoshop and Illustrator. Firefly has shown strong early traction, generating $125 million in annualized recurring revenue, with management expecting that figure to double by year-end. While modest in size relative to Adobe’s total revenue, Firefly’s monetization strategy is still in its early innings, with further potential through upselling, usage-based pricing and expanded use cases. Beyond monetization, AI integration enhances Adobe’s long-term competitive moat through product functionality, stronger customer engagement and increased switching costs. Adobe’s unique access to proprietary data, content workflows and creative content allows it to fine-tune models that serve the high-end needs of professionals—capabilities that generic AI models lack. Strategic partnerships with Microsoft (e.g., Firefly in Microsoft 365 Copilot) and ongoing momentum in Adobe Express further extend its reach into new user segments. Ultimately, we believe Adobe has a durable competitive advantage, underpinned by a large installed base, subscription-led business model, strong brand equity and a long track record of innovation. While short-term concerns over AI disruption have weighed on the stock price, we believe Adobe is well-positioned to harness AI as a driver of value rather than being displaced by it.

Sony, the global leader in video games, image sensors, music and movies, was the top contributor for the period. The company delivered strong quarterly results, driven primarily by its gaming and music businesses, and announced a new executive leadership structure. In gaming, Sony reported a record-high 129 million monthly active users, a 20% year-over-year increase in PlayStation Plus revenue and an expanding user base, as 40% of new PS5 console buyers were new to the platform. The Music segment also continued to benefit from global streaming tailwinds, delivering double-digit profit growth. In a significant leadership transition, Sony announced that, effective April 1, 2025, Hiroki Totoki, currently COO and CFO, would succeed Kenichiro Yoshida as CEO. Our original investment in Sony was grounded in the strategic transformation led by Yoshida-san, where Totoki-san was an instrumental partner in driving Sony’s pivot away from commoditized businesses whilespearheading investments in content IP and semiconductors. Looking ahead, we continue to see opportunity for Sony to capitalize on its unique position as both a content creator and platform owner. The company’s ability to integrate gaming, music, anime and film and leverage IP across platforms (e.g., Crunchyroll and its recent partnership with Kadokawa) should position it well for long-term value creation.

Recent Portfolio Activity

Buys Sells
Air Products and Chemicals (APD) Honeywell (HON)
Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) Michelin (OTCPK:MGDDF)
Millrose Properties (MRP)

During the quarter, we sold our positions in Honeywell, Michelin and Millrose Properties and invested in Air Products and Chemicals and Alphabet.

We first invested in Honeywell, the multinational industrial conglomerate, in the third quarter of 2021. Throughout our ownership period, the company made meaningful progress in its transformation into a modern, innovation-driven enterprise, with a focus on energy efficiency, productivity and connectivity—commonly referred to as the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). Supported by a disciplined capital allocation strategy, Honeywell continued to reshape its product portfolio, emphasizing higher-margin, technology-enabled offerings, such as aerospace software and industrial automation—catalysts we had previously identified. As one of the last remaining diversified U.S. conglomerates, and following years of pressure from activist investors, Honeywell announced in February its decision to split into three independent, publicly listed companies: Honeywell Automation, Honeywell Aerospace and Advanced Materials. We believe this move has the potential to enhance operational focus and unlock shareholder value, in line with similar breakups by other large conglomerates. However, the process is expected to take time and is not anticipated to be completed until the second half of 2026. Given this long runway and the limited near-term visibility, we elected to exit our stake in Honeywell and will continue to monitor the business as further details emerge. We used the proceeds from the sale to fund what we believe is a more attractive investment in Alphabet.

Our team initially invested in Michelin, one of the world’s largest tire manufacturers, in the first quarter of 2021. In our view, the company benefits from a strong competitive position driven by its global scale, brand strength and continued leadership in cutting-edge tire technology. We believe Michelin is well-positioned to enhance profitability through an ongoing shift in its product mix toward higher-margin specialty and large-diameter tires, along with improved SG&A efficiency supported by process optimization initiatives. While Michelin continues to meet all of our core investment criteria, we chose to exit our position in order to fund what we believe is a more compelling opportunity in Air Products and Chemicals.

We received shares of Millrose Properties after it was spun off from Lennar on February 7. Millrose is a new publicly traded company focused on land banking and development. As part of the spinoff, Lennar transferred $6 billion worth of land inventory to Millrose. We believe this spinoff will allow Lennar to be a more capital efficient homebuilder, as less land inventory should lead to stronger FREE cash flow generation and better returns on invested capital. As such, we decided to sell our shares of Millrose and continue with our investment in Lennar’s core homebuilding operations, which we view as a more optimal investment.

Alphabet, Inc.

Headquartered in Mountain View, California and founded by Larry Page and Sergey Brin, Alphabet is one of the world’s most dominant and innovative technology companies. Best known as the parent company of Google, Alphabet generates most of its revenue from digital advertising, particularly search. Google currently holds an estimated 87% market share in U.S. search and nearly 90% globally, underpinning a highly profitable ad business that accounts for roughly 75% of Alphabet’s total revenue.

While Google was founded in 1998 and became public in 2004, Alphabet was created in 2015 to provide greater transparency and operational independence across its varied business lines. Beyond its core, the company has increasingly diversified into accelerating products, including Google Cloud and YouTube’s suite of subscription services (YouTube Premium, YouTube TV and YouTube Music). Some of the quality characteristics we have identified for Alphabet include:

  • Unrivaled scale in global search and digital advertising, protected by powerful network effects and vast proprietary data;
  • An integrated ecosystem—across Search, YouTube, Android, Chrome and Gmail—that supports user retention and ad targeting efficiency;
  • Category leadership in digital media, with YouTube generating over $45 billion in revenue in 2024 and expanding rapidly through ad-supported and subscription models;
  • Emerging strength in cloud computing, with Google Cloud now profitable and scaling meaningfully; and
  • A culture of innovation, supported by its Other Bets incubator, which allows Alphabet to invest in moonshot ideas while maintaining financial discipline.

Attractive Valuation

We believe shares of Alphabet are significantly undervalued at less than 12x our estimate of normalized earnings. The company continues to scale high-margin businesses like Google Cloud and YouTube’s subscription offerings while maintaining robust FREE cash flow generation from its dominant advertising segment.

Compelling Catalysts

Catalysts we have identified for Alphabet, which we believe will cause its stock price to appreciate over our three- to five-year investment horizon, include:

  • Sustained leadership in search and digital advertising, reinforced by Google’s unmatched first-party data and adtech platform;
  • Improving profitability, margin expansion and market share gains for Google Cloud as it effectively competes at scale with AWS and Microsoft Azure; and
  • Continued growth in YouTube subscription revenues as YouTube TV—which is on track to become the largest U.S. pay-TV provider by 2026—captures share from traditional cable providers and premium, ad-free content attracts a broader audience.

Potential Future Catalyst: Alphabet’s deep expertise and resources in AI, particularly through the Gemini model family (the company’s flagship large language model), could enhance monetization across Ads, Search and Cloud. Though this is not explicitly included in our valuation estimates, we view the possibility as a “free option.”

Conclusion

As we progress through the early stages of 2025, the convergence of persistent macroeconomic forces with the renewed presence of trade conflicts has added to the uncertainty facing equity markets. While there certainly was no shortage of headlines during the quarter, we believe trying to time the market or to predict the impact of these developments is a futile task. Instead, our focus remains on evaluating whether these events are truly analyzable, materially differentiated and meaningful to long-term investors—or simply noise that fuels short-term speculation.

At Aristotle Capital, we do not aim to capture short-term gains by “trading” portfolios based on the news of the day. Rather, we remain committed to identifying companies we believe exhibit high-quality characteristics and the resilience to perform across full market cycles. In our experience, breaking news is often fleeting and not quite as impactful as many market participants believe it to be. On the contrary, during periods of economic uncertainty high-quality companies are oftentimes able to make decisions that result in market share gains, thus, potentially, increasing their longer-term intrinsic worth. As such, we will continue to study the microeconomic decisions of individual businesses rather than attempt to predict macroeconomic events or outcomes.

data.title: Mitchells & Butlers: Potential To Hit A Higher Bar
data.description:
In what we deem to be quite a surprising outcome, Mitchells & Butlers (OTCPK:MBPFF) stock is 'only' down by 35.5% from its 2021 high － unlike many of its pub peers who have seen crashes of more than 60.0%. So, with most of the stock's ratios trading at a 5-year low along with a promising growth outlook, we are of the belief that the shares may be worth taking a look at.

Setting the Bar High

M&B's start to its new year hasn't been too shabby at all. Its Q1 figures impressed, beating consensus estimates, as the company's LFL sales grew 3.9% － akin to last year's 4.0%. Although detractors will point to the flat performance, it's worth noting that this comes on the back of a disinflationary environment and tough comps.

More promisingly, the firm had quite a stellar Christmas period. LFL sales growth posted a stunning 10.4%, performing well ahead of the market, while hitting records across its portfolio. This is an impressive feat considering several of its other competitors like Spoons (OTCPK:JDWPF) and Shepherd Neame saw more modest gains of 6.1% and 7.4%, respectively, over a similar period.

Nonetheless, this shouldn't come as a surprise, given M&B's strong record of beating estimates. The numbers also show that its continued initiatives to upgrade and enhance its customer proposition are paying off, having executed over 200 remodels and conversions in the past year. This has been further boosted by the recent acquisitions of Ego, as well as Pesto Restaurants.

Hence, it was positive to see management reiterate its confidence for further strength in "underlying sales growth" for the rest of the year. But like all businesses since the infamous Autumn Budget, the board has warned of a c.£100MM labour cost headwind to come from the hikes in national insurance and national living wage.

Indeed, this will have a profound impact on M&B's costs given its proximity to lower wages, given the industry it operates in, making wage increases more noticeable. All in all, this would add c.4.3% to the pub chain's operating costs from FY24, which is anticipated to trim its EBIT margin by 32bps and another 18bps in FY26, if the latest consensus estimates are to be believed.

The Right Platform to Build On

Nevertheless, CEO Phil Uran expressed his assurance that M&B will be able to handle the £100MM headwind. He even pointed to higher profits this year from a combination of volume accretion and cost efficiencies. This will come as music to investors' ears, with markets having projected a c.£22MM tick up in PBT, as the group continues to leverage on its Ignite programme.

"We continue to leverage the strength of our diverse portfolio of established brands and enviable estate locations and believe we are well positioned to further grow profitability and market share in the year ahead.”

CEO Phil Urban

In tandem with this, the FTSE 250 constituent's workforce has also been robust, with employee turnover dropping to 64.0% from what had been a previously eye-watering 81.0%. This has, therefore, reduced recruitment costs and labour inefficiencies, giving M&B better scope to hold on to its comparatively delicate margins.

Meanwhile, the Ignite programme is enhancing customer engagement, as satisfaction has improved quite a bit. M&B's online platform now gives customers the ability to manage their bookings, place orders, and even receive special offers. Paired with enhanced dish availability and energy efficiencies, and it's no wonder consensus is expecting its bottom line to benefit.

These initiatives, alongside M&B's strong branding, gives it the potential to push margins higher over the medium term when conditions become more favourable. Plus, further efficiencies will likely be realised in the coming years from its Ignite programme, as its brands reap the rewards of revamping its estates, likely encouraging higher revisitation rates and spend per head.

Therefore, the conglomerate has been gaining further market share, as it capitalises on peers like Spoons who have been downsizing amidst poorer profitability. Thus, we anticipate further share growth as the overall demand backdrop for the UK pub scene remains resilient, thanks to a combination of low unemployment and positive real wage growth, for the time being.

Cheap Pick for Premium Returns?

Still, there are risks to consider. Most prominently, M&B may have to pass on the higher costs, which may not sit well with customers, and could slow its market share growth. That being said, the higher ups didn't explicitly mention such a prospect in its FY24 results, which may allude to them allowing higher volumes and the Ignite programme to offset the margin pullback, instead.

Be that as it may, the longer-term outlook for the UK's pub industry doesn't look particularly bright. Consecutive governments have had a bad reputation of hammering the industry with a slew of ever-growing taxes. These include the likes of alcohol duty, sugar tax, and a lack of VAT exemptions amongst many others － all of which eat more and more into pubs' margins.

Despite that, M&B has to deal with the hand they're dealt with, and we have conviction they'll be one of the bigger winners amongst the competition, especially in the budget-to-mid-market space. As such, the fact that the stock is trading at half of its book value is puzzling, to say the least; more so when the earnings growth path is encouraging relative to its current share price.

This may be down to the market being more cautious for the aforementioned reasons. After all, there's no guarantee that pubs will see the strong demand they have over recent years. However, we like the fact that debt levels have come down and that its pension scheme is now in a surplus, thereby freeing up more capital for future buybacks and/or dividends.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests upside of c.70.7%. This is derived by comparing M&B's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model M&B Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
EV/EBITDA (10% wt) 6.1 13.4 8.9 387p
P/E (10% wt) 8.5 35.7 8.5 552p
FP/E (25% wt) 7.7 21.7 14.1 494p
PEG (55% wt) 1.0 1.3 1.1 261p
Fair Value 360p

***As a reminder to our readers, Mitchells & Butlers's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:MAB). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios with an emphasis on forward-looking earnings multiples:

  • EV/EBITDA (10% wt);
  • P/E (10% wt);
  • Forward P/E (25% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (55% wt);

We prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations rather than past performance. Although, we include trailing ratios with lower weightings in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. EV/EBITDA (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to FY24 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Movement in the Valuation of the Property Portfolio + Depreciation of Property, Plant, and Equipment + Depreciation of Right-of-Use Assets + Amortisation of Intangibles.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Restaurant/Dining sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  2. P/E (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to FY24 results.
    • EPS is calculated from basic EPS on profit after tax, divided by basic shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Restaurant/Dining sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  3. FP/E (25% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections, if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1'25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1'25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS / 100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from basic EPS on FTM profit after tax, divided by projected basic shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Restaurant/Dining sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  4. FPEG (55% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS / TTM EPS) ^ (1 / 3 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price / TTM EPS) / CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Restaurant/Dining sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.

data.title: SQQQ: April 9th Was A Major Red Flag
data.description:
April 9th, 2024 produced a 12.2% return in the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) in a single market session. It was the second largest single-day rally in the index in history. At the same time, the ProShares UltraShort QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:SQQQ) - a fund designed to benefit from declines in the Qs - fell by a staggering 35.3%. In this article, we'll get into what the SQQQ does, who it is designed for, and why I think this financial product is going to see more explosive days (and opportunities) in the months to come.

SQQQ Details

  • AUM: $2.24 billion
  • Expense Ratio: 0.95%
  • Inception: 02/09/2010

The ProShares UltraShort QQQ ETF is how tactical traders can benefit from a falling Nasdaq-100. SQQQ is leveraged 3x and re-balanced daily. So if the Nasdaq-100 goes down by 2% in a single day, SQQQ holders can expect to generate a positive 6% return in the same day. Perhaps unsurprisingly, betting against a market that has rallied to the degree the Nasdaq has in recent years has resulted in catastrophic losses for anyone who has held the ticker as a long-term speculation. Over the last five years, the price of SQQQ has fallen by 98%:

This is partially due to the nature of a fund that bets against a secular bull market. But it's also indicative of how volatile and risky holding a product that is not designed to be hold long term can be. Consider how SQQ performed during 2022:

Here we see a fund that provided traders with absolutely explosive moves higher but that deeply corrected when the broader market experienced relief rallies - such as the one that we are likely witnessing as of article submission.

I've covered these kinds of leveraged products for Seeking Alpha before. I cannot stress this enough; these types of funds are not suitable for holding long term. In fact, I wouldn't even attempt to hold them for longer than a couple of weeks, at most. Gains in SQQQ can be magnificent when the timing is impeccable and the trader holding the fund gets several consecutive down days in the index. Chop will eat shareholders alive, and broad market gains will destroy capital sitting in SQQQ very quickly.

Given all of this, I will not call SQQQ a 'buy.' Even in the event that we're in a deeper bear market rather than just a standard correction, I would still not hold SQQQ for more than a few days at a time due to the 3x leverage. That said, I think traders will have several good opportunities to play with SQQQ in the future.

April 9th Likely Signals More Red Ahead

As mentioned in the introduction of this article, April 9th was the 2nd best day for the Nasdaq ever by a single session percentage return. At 1,932 points, it was also the largest day every by intraday point swing after dethroning the short-lived reign of April 7th, 2025. Much of what has been happening in markets has been event-driven through headlines that have meticulously followed every change in the ongoing tariff saga.

Getting past whatever comes out of these potential tariffs, I think there is a much more ominous signal April 9th is delivering to the market. Despite optimism with tariffs, the pain for market optimists may not be over just yet.

Rank Date Close Net Change % Change
1 2001-01-03 2,616.69 324.83 14.17
2 2025-04-09 17,124.97 1,857.06 12.16
3 2008-10-13 1,844.25 194.74 11.81
4 2000-12-05 2,889.80 274.05 10.48
5 2008-10-28 1,649.47 143.57 9.53

Source: Wikipedia

The table above shows the top 5 days for the Nasdaq by single day percentage gain. Every other day in this table has come during a deeper bear market. The fourth and first ranked gaining days came within a month of each other. January 3rd, 2001 preceded a brief relief rally that quickly reverted lower. Within three months, the market had fallen 45% from the 1/03/01 close.

The bottom in the Nasdaq didn't come until Q4 2002. And it isn't just the dot-com era sessions from the table in this section that I find concerning. The October 2008 sessions also preceded further declines:

Here we again see these large green candles, followed by further declines in the market. October 13th, 2008 - the third-best single day performance for the Nasdaq - preceded another 29% decline over the next month. Again, we see a historic green candle during that decline; October 28th, 2008 - the fifth-best single day Nasdaq performance - came just two weeks after the third-best day for the Qs and served primarily as a relief rally during the GFC. While these instances were far closer to the bottom in the Nasdaq than the dot-com era instances, the reality it is still took 7 months for the QQQ to close above the October 13th, 2008 session.

It isn't shown in the table above, but the 6th best day for the Nasdaq came on March 13th, 2020. That session preceded the worst single day Nasdaq performance ever on March 16th, 2020 when the index fell by 12.3%. Obviously, what I'm sharing are all extreme outliers from typical market behavior. But that's kind of the point. What we saw on April 9th was not normal, and it is indicative of increasing market volatility.

My gut feel is that what the market is experiencing with tariff concerns is going to ultimately be a closer fit with the dot-com era instances of large single day gains rather than the GFC-era versions. The latter of which came closer to the end of the bear market.

Consider how 'growth' has performed against 'value' over the last 15 years. Growth outperformed value significantly during the late-90's dot-com run. That didn't happen prior to the GFC meltdown in stocks. Yet, the post-COVID growth rocket-ship ride is far more similar to what happened during dot-com. Thus, I think the Nasdaq is in for a rough ride over the medium term.

Closing Takeaway

If history is a guide, the performance of the Nasdaq on April 9th is potentially a strong indication that there are further declines coming in stocks, particularly the kind of high-flying tech stocks that are in the Nasdaq. To be clear, I do actually think we are likely to experience a meaningful rally in the US equity market for the remainder of April. And I think there are interesting opportunities from the long side that I've covered for Seeking Alpha in prior work. You can look at some of those ideas here, here, and here.

But speaking more specifically about broad market equities for the remainder of the year, I would be very surprised if the Qs ended 2025 higher than current levels. Thus, I suspect a product like SQQQ will do what it has done in the past; produce days of incredible returns, days of monumental losses, and generally decay if held for more than a few weeks or months. For tactical purposes, have it. Just don't plan to get rich by riding a SQQQ through a deeper bear market that lasts more than a year.

Manager Commentary

“Markets have been running at a fast pace — with the likely consequence of lower market returns over the next decade.” - C Worldwide Asset Management

Market in Review

Global equity markets ended the fourth quarter of 2024 on a subdued note after reaching a high in the wake of the U.S. presidential election. The Federal Reserve (“Fed”) signaled a more cautious policy path, and combined with the unpredictability of Trump’s economic agenda, equity markets gave back some of their earlier gains.

Portfolio Performance

The Harbor International Compounders ETF (“ETF”) returned -9.22% (at NAV) compared to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index, which returned -7.60%. Stock selection within Financials, Health Care, and Industrials detracted the most from relative results. Stock selection within Consumer Discretionary, Materials, and Information Technology were additive to relative results during the period.

From a relative performance perspective, 2024 was a disappointing year. Among the detractors to ETF performance were several consumer companies that continued to struggle with lingering effects of the global pandemic. There should be some relief for these companies in 2025 as inflationary pressures ease. Several large holdings, like ASML (ASML) and Novo Nordisk (NVO), had a difficult second half of 2024 due to what we believe to be temporary setbacks.

Contributors and Detractors

Much of the underperformance during the quarter can be attributed to disappointments in our holdings, especially Novo Nordisk, Atlas Copco (OTCPK:ATLKF), and AIA Group (OTCPK:AAIGF). Novo Nordisk released Phase 3 data on CagriSema, the company’s weight-loss drug, showing weight loss of 22.7% — which was short of the market’s expectation of 25% — causing shares to drop 21%. Even though CagriSema surpassed competitor Eli Lilly’s (LLY) SURMOUNT 1 trial, the nature of the Novo Nordisk trial and results shook investor confidence. Therefore, a share price recovery will likely be gradual.

Among positive contributors in the quarter were technology companies TSMC (TSM), SAP (SAP), and Sony (SONY). The formerly dominant chip producer, Intel, saw its CEO resign, underscoring the differences between the boom TSMC is experiencing and the difficulties Intel is having in moving to the advanced nodes. While there is concern around AI spending going into 2026 and the need for graphics processing units to train the most advanced models, TSMC should have robust growth beyond next year as the need for inference (i.e., using the models) will support the growth of ASIC (application-specific integrated circuits) chips and, eventually, Edge AI.

Sector Underweights and Overweights

Entering the year, the portfolio was notably overweight in Industrials, Information Technology, and Health Care, while it was underweight in Energy, Financials, and Communication Services. As of December 2024, Industrials remain the largest active sector overweight, now representing 29.38% of the portfolio compared to the index's 13.94%.

We employ an unconstrained approach regarding sectors and countries, gravitating toward the best long-term opportunities. Country and sector weights are therefore a residual of stock selection.

Outlook

Donald Trump’s policy choices will influence the global economy and stock markets. His intentions are clear. Trump likes rising stock markets and falling interest rates and wants to “Make America Great Again.” Tariffs as a negotiation tool might be powerful, but time is also a factor. If negotiations become prolonged, there is a risk that MAGA policies will be inflationary and restrictive to growth. There is only so much you can control — even as the President of the United States. Ongoing trade tensions are also a risk factor.

Currently, we are in the honeymoon period of marriage No. 2 with Trump. After the inauguration, it will be back to everyday life — and our everyday life is focused on identifying long-term sustainable earnings growth.

Markets have been running at a fast pace — with the likely consequence of lower market returns over the next decade. However, in relation to market returns, it will be essential to distinguish between the various market indexes. Looking at the broader markets — i.e., the equal-weighted indexes — these have not yielded returns that compare to market cap-weighted indexes. Paradoxically, passive investing has become active given the extreme concentration within market-cap-weighted indices. Suddenly, passive investors face an important choice of which index to follow: What absolute index risk are you comfortable with?

We prefer to know what we invest in. It’s our core philosophy. A maximum of 30 stocks of high-quality, well-positioned companies for the longer term. Some companies in our portfolio have delivered solid annual mid-teen earnings growth for years without being rewarded by the market. This is partly due to the concentration factor. When market leadership broadens, these companies should have significant catch-up potential.

We advocate staying with the long-term mindset and staying invested. The global economy faces risks. However, we see many companies poised for continued earnings growth in the coming years, and barring a negative inflation surprise, the monetary environment should stay accommodative, offering support for equities.

MANAGER COMMENTARY

As of 12/31/2024

“We are beginning to see the impact that the unprecedented global-tightening cycle of the past two-plus years is having on companies.”

Marathon London

Market in Review

Despite several major geopolitical events occurring in the final quarter of 2024, the global market has been remarkably calm. The MSCI World Index, for example, was close to flat over the period. However, like an iceberg — where only the tip is visible above the surface — global index performance hides significant differences in regional trends.

Most markets floundered a little during October. Then came the U.S. election, and the U.S. market — along with neighboring Canada — started an upswing as it became clear that Donald Trump would be returned to the White House. However, mixed macroeconomic news began surfacing in early December and, following the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (Fed) news conference, all markets fell several percentage points. Despite the Fed’s decision to cut rates by 0.25%, as expected, the tone of the conference was hawkish, and forward guidance was for fewer cuts over a longer time frame.

Looking to styles, growth was the winner at the global index level but there were regional differences. North America, Pacific ex-Japan and, to a lesser extent, emerging markets saw growth outperform. In other developed markets, value outperformed. Similarly, large caps outperformed in most regions, while mid-caps did best in Europe and Pacific ex- Japan.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned -7.58%, performing broadly in line with its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Ex.-U.S. (ND) Index, which returned -7.60%.

Stock selection was neutral overall, with the benefit from emerging markets and Canadian positions being countered by underperformance from other regions, particularly Pacific ex-Japan. It’s a similar story at the sector level, with underperformance from stock selection in Industrials and Financials countered by outperformance of selection in Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples. Allocation effects — which are a residual of stock selection rather than a separate target — were muted, where most positions were somewhat positive, but the benefit was largely countered by the underweight to Information Technology.

Contributors & Detractors

Flutter Entertainment (FLUT)(OTC:PDYPY), the Ireland-based gaming company, contributed to performance. The company transferred its primary listing to the New York Stock Exchange early in 2024 but maintains a core holding in Marathon’s European portfolios given its dominant position in the regional sports betting market. The share price benefited when the company revised overall earnings guidance upward. This related primarily to the “ex-U.S.” business, where customers have been spending a little more on gaming as economic conditions eased.

3i Group (OTCPK:TGOPF)(OTCPK:TGOPY), the U.K.-listed, private equity specialist, continued its long-term outperformance as its largest investment, Action, Europe’s largest non-food discount retailer, went from strength to strength. Despite being the strongest performer in the FTSE 100 over the past five years, 3i Group shares still trade on a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of around 8, and Marathon continues to consider the company good value.

Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF), the Denmark-based pharmaceutical firm, detracted from performance during the quarter. The company has performed particularly well since its GLP-1 diabetes drug was found to be useful in combatting obesity in the early 2020s. The company released the results of late-stage trials of its next generation anti-obesity treatment, CagriSema, during the quarter. While positive, the results indicate that the drug is less effective than the company had hoped, and the stock tumbled around 20%.

Vestas Wind Systems (OTCPK:VWSYF)(OTCPK:VWDRY), the Danish wind turbine manufacturer, was also a detractor. Shares plunged in early November due to weak earnings, but also because it became clear that Donald Trump was likely to win the U.S. presidential race. Although it is difficult to assess which had more impact, Trump is openly skeptical of renewable energy and highly supportive of the fossil fuel industry, which may represent an impediment to Vestas’s plans in the market.

Buys & Sells

The Fund acquired AVI, a South African business operating a premium portfolio of beverage, snack, apparel, and personal-care brands. The business has consistently generated high returns — double that of local peers — and is continuously extending its value proposition. The shares have been neglected for some time, and Marathon invested when it was on sale at a low P/E ratio.

We also acquired bioMérieux, a global leader in in-vitro and molecular diagnostics. The company’s share price has come down ~30% since 2020, despite the business’s steady growth. The valuation proved attractive for a business of such quality with good prospects for long-term growth.

Localiza (OTCQX:LZRFY) was sold during the quarter due to deteriorating prospects for the Brazilian car rental business. The firm, now with over 50% market share, is unlikely to make further gains through market consolidation and thus — accompanied by a poor view of a recent acquisition and change in management — Marathon exited the position.

We sold Net One Systems (OTCPK:NONEY)(NONEF), the Japanese investment and communications technology company, after it was taken over by SCSK Corporation in November.

Sector Underweights & Overweights

The Fund’s sector weightings are a by-product of Marathon London’s bottom-up stock selection process, which does not tend to change materially over the course of a quarter or year.

As of Dec. 31, 2024, the largest overweight in the Fund was Industrials and Consumer Discretionary; the largest underweight was Information Technology followed by Communication Services.

Country Allocation

The Fund’s country weightings are a by-product of Marathon London’s bottom-up stock selection process and thus do not tend to change materially over the course of a quarter or year.

As of Dec. 31, 2024, the Fund’s largest overweights were to the U.K. and South Africa, while the largest underweights were to China and Switzerland.

Outlook

Marathon’s approach is long term, with a typical holding period of more than five years. Portfolio managers generally do not take short-term positions predicated on a particular view of the future, and Marathon does not do macroeconomic research, have “house views,” or make any predictions or forecasts. The team continues to seek out companies that, they believe, are well placed to outperform their peers under most potential economic conditions.

That said, one interesting observation has been the effect of extended ultra-low interest rates on Marathon’s approach. Capital cycle analysis seeks opportunities both among high-return-on-capital companies benefiting from a positive (“top-half”) capital cycle and those in a lower return (“bottom-half”) universe. Usually, the bottom half offers opportunities for companies that must consolidate to improve profitability. However, there has been a dearth of opportunity for the Fund in the bottom half, because very low interest rates have allowed “zombie” companies to borrow at minimal cost and continue operating.

The rise in interest rates since 2021-2022 has incrementally generated some of the “creative destruction,” which has been largely lacking in markets since the global financial crisis, and which creates bottom-half opportunities for Marathon’s portfolio managers. However, the lag between interest rate changes and economic impact varies and can be as long as several years. We are beginning to see the impact that the unprecedented global-tightening cycle of the past two-plus years is having on companies. The interest cycle has turned in many countries, however, with rates being cut recently. Nevertheless, due to the lag in impact, we expect to see increased bottom-half opportunity in the coming years.

data.title: Day One Biopharmaceuticals Pullback Is A Potential Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Day One Biopharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:DAWN) received accelerated approval for Ojemda (tovorafenib), for the treatment of relapsed/refractory BRAF-altered pediatric low grade gliomas (pLGG), in April 2024. The launch since has gone fairly well for DAWN with the company reporting $57.2M in net product revenues for 2024 from Ojemda, despite the drug only launching in mid-Q2'24. Shareholders haven't had an easy ride however, perhaps because approval and some sales expectations were already built into the price, and the recent pullback in the biotech sector and market as a whole has DAWN near 52-week lows.

The Ojemda launch so far

With just three quarters of data to look at, it might be hard to estimate what the rate of growth in sales might look like going forward, but DAWN does offer additional insights beyond net product revenues, with the company reporting prescriptions written since launch (1647 prescriptions from launch to year-end 2024).

Indeed, DAWN reported 619 prescriptions in Q3'24, noting an 159% increase from the prior quarter. Combining that with the information that there were 1647 scripts in 2024 allows us to generate a Q4'24 number of approximately 789 scripts.

Table 1: Prescriptions of Ojemda per quarter. Italicized numbers represent Biotech Beast estimates.

Prescriptions Increase vs Prior quarter
Q2'24 ~239
Q3'24 619 159%

Q4'24

 ~789 ~27.5%

2024

 1647

A 27.5% increase in scripts from Q3'24 to Q4'24 still represents underlying growth, even if it doesn't meet the 44% increase in net product revenues seen going from Q3'24 to Q4'24. Indeed, Q4'24 can be a time when there is some seasonality, reducing the number of patients starting on therapy. Overall, I think there is still plenty of room for growth ahead, with DAWN estimating that the addressable US opportunity for Ojemda is ~2000-3000 patients. Further, the drug is already seeing off label use, which, while it may come with a reduced duration of treatment, can still contribute to revenues each quarter.

A wait for more data?

While clinical catalysts could provide stimulus for a rally or fall in DAWN's stock, the company is still a while away from the most obvious clinical catalysts. For example, FIREFLY-2, the company's study of Ojemda in front-line pLGG patients. That study is not expected to reach full enrollment until H1'26, so unless there is an early success on an interim analysis, then it doesn't look like a near-term catalyst.

As for DAY301, DAWN's anti-PTK7 antibody-drug conjugate (ADC), the company only got underway with clinical work recently, and the fact that the CEO seemed to suggest they might not disclose data, perhaps even until they have some dose expansion data, could mean we may be waiting a while for an update.

The plan for us as it relates to disclosure of 301 is to ensure that we have a sufficient data set that allows for the external world, investors, you all and the physician community to understand our development plan and the underlying rationale for it. And for that reason, I anticipate that certainly, we'll need additional time and data from the Phase Ia portion or the dose escalation portion of the trial, but potentially also some dose expansion or dose cohort expansion data as well. And we haven't yet guided specifically around the timing of when those two data sets could be available.

Jeremy Bender, CEO of DAWN, Q4'24 earnings call.

In any case, even if DAWN sees strong data during dose escalation, and doesn't wait until it also has some dose expansion data to share some results, it still might take a few quarters to hear how DAY301 is performing.

Financial Overview

DAWN reported net product revenues of $29.0M for Q4'24, up from $20.1M in Q3'24. The company finished 2024 with $531.7M in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments. R&D expenses were $61.8M in Q4'24, with SG&A expenses of $29.8M in the same quarter. DAWN reported a net loss of $65.7M in Q4'24 with net cash used in operating activities of $78.1M in 2024, although this latter number is aided by the fact that DAWN brought in $108M from the sale of a priority review voucher.

There were 101,354,516 shares of DAWN's common stock outstanding as of February 20, 2025. DAWN has a market cap of $670M ($6.62 per share).

Analyst estimates for the next four years see DAWN producing revenues of over $500M by 2028. Indeed, DAWN currently trades at a forward price/sales of 5.04 based on 2025 revenue estimates of $161.7M, even though the drug will be in its first full year of sales in 2025.

DAWN looks even more compelling in terms of EV/sales estimates, for example, a forward EV/Sales of 1.28, thanks to its recent pullback towards cash value. That being said, it is that true the company will continue to burn cash going forward. DAWN's 10-K notes at least 12 months of cash runway, and I don't think a near-term cash raise is likely, but the company's cash may still continue to run down for several quarters as sales of Ojemda hopefully continue to increase.

Conclusions, rating and risks

DAWN's Ojemda is off to a good start and there should still be room for further growth in the current indication, and perhaps somewhat with off label use, although success in new indications, such as in FIREFLY-2, might take a while. The same can be said regarding DAY301, some investor patience may be required. Nonetheless, the recent pullback has the company's valuation looking pretty cheap and for those with a few quarters of patience, readouts from FIREFLY-2 and the DAY301 work will then become nearer-term catalysts. I currently rate DAWN a buy (I'm long the stock and may purchase more).

There are multiple risks of any long position in DAWN, a few of which I'll discuss here. Firstly, DAWN could experience a drop in off label use of Ojemda as physicians gain experience with its use and might not be impressed with the drug in those indications.

Secondly, DAWN could report middling sales growth for Q1'25 or with subsequent earnings announcements, leading to analyst downgrades and share price drops.

Lastly, DAWN could report toxicity issues with DAY301, triggering the end of its development, or even just delays as the company attempts to address the issue. I have no reason to believe this will be the case currently, but it is always a possibility for a new drug that can't be ruled out.

data.title: EVT: Funding Retirement With Tax-Efficient Dividend Income
data.description:
Overview

The recent market decline has presented many attractive opportunities for income investors. Many high-quality funds now trade at a discounted valuation and offer a superior dividend yield. I believe that Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (NYSE:EVT) is one of these high-quality funds that currently presents an attractive entry opportunity. I previously covered EVT and issued a buy rating due to the consistent performance and tax-efficient distribution income. Since the time of my last coverage, EVT has released a new annual report, which prompted me to revisit this fund to monitor the performance.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, EVT's price has declined by more than 10.7%, with most of the decline taking place over the last week. Market indexes declined in response to President Trump's tariff proposal. As a result, EVT now trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return is still a loss of 5.3% due to the recent decline. However, EVT is an attractive fund because of the starting dividend yield of 9.7% and the fact that distributions are paid out on a monthly basis.

EVT has a public inception dating back to 2003, meaning the fund has over two decades worth of performance data that we can reference. One of the most notable aspects of EVT's history is the resilience through market downturns and ability to preserve investor capital over a long period of time. The ability to preserve capital while simultaneously providing investors with attractive total returns is what also makes EVT a great fund for retired investors looking to create a reliable stream of supplemental income. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy and holdings that EVT utilizes to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, EVT has total net assets of about $1.9B that is spread across 81 different holdings. I like that the fund maintains a focus on diversity, with Financials making up the largest slice of the fund at 29.46%. This is followed by a weight in Health Care and Industrial companies, accounting for 15% and 13.04% of assets respectively.

Part of the fund's strategy also includes about a 20% exposure to fixed income investments. This includes things like investment grade bonds, high yield credit investments, and preferred securities. This can help stabilize the fund's price and make it a little more resilient to highly volatile market conditions. The focus on fixed income investments means that EVT has the potential to be less reliant on bullish market momentum to fuel growth, since it can still generate positive income through declines.

Looking at the top ten holdings, we can see that JPMorgan (JPM) is the largest holding at 3.93%. This is followed by a 2.29% weight to Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) and a 2.06% weight to Walt Disney (DIS). EVT doesn't implement any fancy option strategies against the underlying holdings, making the fund a very straightforward option that can partake in bullish market movements. The only reason that EVT's long-term price history doesn't show any meaningful level of capital appreciation is because the fund essentially pays out all the gains as a distribution.

Lastly, EVT does implement a conservative amount of leverage as part of its strategy. Leverage currently amounts to 18.9% of the fund's assets, which is used in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Ideally, I'd like for leverage to stay below the 20% level in this higher interest rate environment. A conservative use of leverage here is best during a period where the cost of borrowing is near the highest range it's been over the last decade.

Updated Outlook & Valuation

Following the most recent price decline, EVT now trades at a discount to NAV of about 10.9%. For reference, the price has traded at an average discount to NAV of only 5.8% over the last three-year period, indicating that this can be an attractive entry point for new shareholders, as well as an ideal time to continue accumulating for existing shareholders. Looking back over the last decade, every time EVT has reached a discount level in this range, the price has eventually recovered back up to fair value. Since the fund is diversified across holdings and sectors, I have confidence that this pattern can repeat itself in the future, as it has over the last two decades.

Since a portion of the portfolio is exposed to fixed income investments, I believe that a lower interest rate environment can be a catalyst for growth. For instance, the Fed reduced interest rates to near zero levels in response to the pandemic in 2020 and 2021. As a result, this is when EVT saw the highest level of net investment income. A lower interest rate meant that it was possible for companies to acquire cheap debt financing. For income-oriented investments, it was an attractive time of affordable debt that made it a lot easier for valuations to increase.

Conversely, a period of higher interest rates increases the cost of debt, which increases the odds of borrower defaults and creates an environment where there are less companies seeking capital. Companies tend to focus on capital efficiency and cost reduction during periods of elevated interest rates, which can delay the pursuit of growth initiatives. This usually results in slower growth over time. Unfortunately, the Fed has decided to leave interest rates unchanged for now, due to stubbornly high inflationary data. While the Fed does estimate that there will be one interest rate cut to take place over 2025, it's hard to determine whether or not this will really drive the needle forward for growth. While Trump continues to put pressure on the Fed to cut rates, it's still questionable whether or not interest rate cuts make sense yet for the Fed.

What I like about EVT is the consistent dividend payout history over the last decade. The fund has been able to maintain consistent payouts throughout different market conditions, which shows that management values the dividend consistency for shareholders. Despite the lack of raises over the last decade, the consistent payouts enable investors to essentially create their own dividend income growth by reinvesting portions of their distributions.

Another attractive aspect of EVT is that the majority of the distributions are tax-efficient. Since the fund doesn't utilize any fancy option strategies against its holdings, most of the distributions seem to be funded by realized long-term capital gains, according to the most recent section19(a) notice. We can see that 79% of the distributions YTD have been funded by net realized long-term gains, while the remaining portion were funded by either net investment income or short-term realized gains.

Long term capital gains have favorable tax consequences, making the fund a bit more flexible in terms of what accounts EVT can be utilized within. However, investors should still expect to owe taxes on the distributions received, since net investment income and short-term capital gains are usually classified as ordinary income. These concerns can be offset by simply utilizing EVT within a tax advantaged account, but I would consult with a professional tax consultant regarding your specific circumstance.

Risk & Resilience

Since EVT has been around for over two decades, we can see how the fund has performed through other market downturns. EVT's total price return since inception is still slightly positive at 3.2%. This means that EVT has been able to efficiently recover its NAV through every market crisis over the last two decades, such as the Great Financial Crisis in 2008 and the Covid crash in 2020. Through every one of these instances, EVT has been able to preserve investor capital, which is extremely valuable to retirees. Even through these market downturns, EVT has a total return of nearly 440% since inception.

Additionally, EVT has proven to be more resilient through recent market downturns. For instance, markets declined throughout 2022 due to a Fed rate shock and record-breaking inflation. EVT's consistent distributions helped outperform both the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq throughout 2022.

The obvious risk of a position in EVT is that shareholders are likely to underperform traditional index-tracking ETFs such as SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) over the long term. For instance, a quick comparison reveals that EVT severely underperforms both of these ETFs since its inception, despite having a large dividend yield. If you are a younger investor with plenty of time ahead, you are likely better off with a more traditional ETF that doesn't prioritize income.

Takeawayb

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on EVT to a strong buy due to the attractive valuation and the fund's consistent ability for capital preservation over a long period of time. The fund currently trades at a discount to NAV of 10.9%, which is a rare opportunity seen over the last decade. The fund has also demonstrated consistent NAV growth over time, which proves the strategy's efficiency at capitalizing during bull markets. Similarly, EVT has been more resilient than traditional indexes during market declines, due to the consistent distributions paid out. The tax-efficient manner of the distributions also increases the appeal for retirees.
